# **YARN Queue Plan for FY26Q1**

Status: *Approved*  Author: [Yi Chen](mailto:yichen@stripe.com)    Created: Jan 8, 2025  Last Updated: Jan 22, 2025

| Stakeholder | Role | Comments |
| :---- | :---- | :---- |
| [Alexander Bohr](mailto:abohr@salesforce.com) | DIG DRI |  This all sounds good,  let’s just make sure to sync the week these go on and go into that week with some baselines of our biggest jobs and see how their run time does week over week.  I think the existing EMR grafana charts and the Tableau dashboard for spark performance will be enough to get a baseline and compare WoW |
| [Jake Ziegler](mailto:jacob.ziegler@salesforce.com) | MF DRI | Agreed, that it all sounds good.  |

# Summary

In FY26Q1, we plan to begin addressing the DIG and MF teams’ urgent need for capacity guarantees for their critical Spark workloads\[1\]. This is a sub project under the umbrella project of Spark Capacity Management ([Spark Capacity Management: Business Requirements](https://docs.google.com/document/u/0/d/1bmYSUSdyJcyuvmUvK-tR1ddFe1PlcZXBF6cPKeTWFsk/edit)). When this project is fully complete, the DIG team will have moved all of their workload in the prod-data-platform-emr6 cluster under a top-level queue, named “team-data-ingestion”, and the DIG team can allocate the queue resources in child-queues with easy control. Similarly, the MF team will have moved all of the critical workload into a top-level queue named “team-metric-foundation” in the “prod-dma-emr6” cluster, with full control on how to allocate resources within its child-queues.

There is no plan to change the FairScheduler to CapacityScheduler[\[2\]](#bookmark=id.m401703mc4ng), and no plan to add or change other queues in Q1[\[1\]](#bookmark=id.my6jraksxfoq)[\[3\]](#bookmark=id.va84mnqiny2y)[\[4\]](#bookmark=id.vlb4g4714hya). 

# Queue Design

## Team-specific top-level queues

We plan to name the queues that match the owner team’s Slack channel name. This is because we already have a tradition of mapping job owners to a team’s Slack channel name. Usually the owner teams will have a team channel, and sometimes a dedicated escal channel. The names can be changed easily in the future, so we opt to the more descriptive team channel name instead of the escal channel name.

We will create a top-level queue named team-data-ingestion for DIG’s critical workloads running in the prod-data-platform-emr6 cluster and team-metrics-foundations for critical workloads running in the prod-dma-emr6 cluster.

## Static YARN queue allocation files

Today, all EMR clusters are sharing the same template file for the queue allocation definition. As part of Q1, we will use static YARN queue allocation files for the prod-data-platform-emr6 and prod-dma-emr6 clusters. This allows the queue owners (DIG and MF teams) to manage their own sub-queues. For example, the DIG and MF teams can define the child-queues anyway they want. They could name it based on priority (e.g. tier1, tier2), or based on more descriptive names (e.g. DAG name).

## No Preemption

We do not plan to enable preemption for the top-level team-specific queues in Q1, as our priority is ensuring the reliability of these critical workloads[\[4\]](#bookmark=id.vlb4g4714hya). Preemption comes with risks, but we can achieve our goals  without these risks for Q1[\[5\]](#bookmark=id.w0y3vfrhuomq). We will re-evaluate this decision at a later time after we gain operational experiences.

## Ownership Boundaries

The DI team is responsible for the following:

* Defining and tuning the minimum and maximum queue capacity for the top-level queues  
* Supporting the queue owners during the migration process

The queue owners (e.g. DIG and MF) are responsible for:

* Migrating existing workload under the new team-specific top-level queue.  
  * All Spark jobs owned by the DIG team should be moved under the team-data-ingestion queue.  
  * All Spark jobs owned by the MF team should be moved under the team-metric-foundations queue.  
* Define sub queues under the top-level queues, and allocate resources between them.   
* Gain operational knowledge of YARN queues, gain workload allocation insights of their own workload, and be able to use these insights to define a reasonable sub-queue allocation. 

# Execution Plan

## DIG (prod-data-platform-emr6) Plan

* We will create a new top-level queue, named “team-data-ingestion”, with a max capacity that matches the current prod-data-platform-emr6 cluster.   
  * We will work with the DIG team and find an DRI that represents the DIG team, who will be responsible for identifying all workloads running in the prod-data-platform-emr6 cluster, and migrate them under the new “team-data-ingestion” queue.  
  * DIG team DRI should help identify all the DAGs in the “prod-data-platform-emr6” cluster before moving them to the “team-data-ingestion” queue. This is because we need to analyze the current resource needs before the move so that the new queue has the appropriate resources.  
  * Based on the historical resource needs of the DAGS owned by DIG in the cluster, DI team will create a weight for the team-data-ingestion queue. For example, if the identified DAGs consume about 80% of the data-platform-emr6 cluster consistently, we will weight this queue to ensure (with soft enforcement) that the team-data-ingestion will get about 80% of the cluster resources.  
  * After the queue weight is defined based on historical data, the DIG DRI will change the queue configurations and move these identified workloads to the team-data-ingestion queue.  
  * \[optional\] DIG team DRI can create sub-queues within the team-data-ingestion queue to provide resource guarantees for jobs with SLA needs. For example, under the team-data-ingestion queue, the DIG team can create a sub-queue named “critical”, and provision enough resources for clog and flog\_by\_api DAGs, with an appropriate weight to when these jobs are running they will be able to obtain resources. Note that DIG team DRI is responsible for balancing the queue resources among all other DAGs in the team queue. If more resources are allocated to flog\_by\_api and clogs DAGs, other dags will have less resources when these big jobs are running.

## MF (prod-dma-emr6) Plan

* We will repeat the above steps for “team-metric-foundation” in the prod-dma-emr6 cluster.  
* The prod-dma-emr6 cluster has a more bursty workload with more auto-scaling actions, so I anticipate more work for tuning the queue and cluster configuration, and may take a few iterations.

# Appendix

## \[1\] Why are we introducing queues only to the two clusters for the two teams?

Implementing YARN queues is an interactive process: there is no such a thing that we can get it right in the first try. We will need to learn more about our workload during the queue onboarding process. Considering the resource constraints, and the hard deadline of migrating all workload to EMR 7 ([Jan 25, 2026](https://slack-pde.slack.com/archives/CJFHE7AR3/p1734044872790929)), we need to scope down the YARN queue project in FY26.

## \[2\] Why don't we change FairScheduler to CapacityScheduler?

It’s still in the backlog, but we will delay that decision. This is because CapacityScheduler does not offer additional benefits over FairScheduler for our needs in Q1, which will suffice to provide material relief for the DIG and MF teams. We will reevaluate this decision after operating the queues for the DIG and MF teams for a few months and potentially during the EMR 7 upgrades.

## \[3\] What should we do with all other workloads not owned by the DIG and MF teams?

We will keep them unchanged in Q1, as there are currently no differences between the production queues and the default queues. The only existing queue, the “critical” queue, may be replaced by the team-specific top-level queues for MF and DIG.

## \[4\] Why don't we implement or design the queue structure now?

Currently, our queue configuration is essentially non-existent. At present, all clusters follow the same template for queue allocation, with queues named “prod,” “backfill,” “livy,” and similar. However, these queues, with one exception, are merely labels and have no functional impact. The sole exception is the “critical” queue, which includes a capacity guarantee.

Queue organization cannot be approached as a one-time effort; it must be an iterative process, as there is no universal solution that fits all scenarios. Given the current state of our system, we lack insights and operational knowledge about our workload, making it crucial to proceed cautiously and incrementally. Additionally, many advanced queue features, such as preemption, come with inherent risks. I anticipate that we will see temporary escals and issues during this interactive process, and this process will take six months to a year and will likely overlap with our EMR 7 upgrade.

## \[5\] What to expect with pre-emption disabled in Q1?

Pre-emption comes with risks, and we have more tools available to achieve the same desired outcomes without enabling pre-emption in Q1. With preemption disabled, we can still ensure a material impact for reasonable resource guarantees. For example, if the DIG queue is temporarily under-resourced, we can be confident that, in most cases, it will scale up within an hour. Additionally, we can be highly confident that the queue will receive its guaranteed resources within five hours. This is because the queue can still acquire resources as applications in over-resourced queues complete.

The following chart illustrates that, in the data-platform cluster, more than 80% of applications finish within an hour.

![][image1]

## \[6\] Should we merge the Percolator cluster into the Data Platform cluster in Q1?

The percolator cluster is running Java 8 while the data-platform cluster is running Java 11\. It may not be a good idea to merge these two clusters in Q1 due to the Java version issue. We may consider this as a stretch goal, or delay after Q1 when we are doing EMR 7 upgrades. It may be easier to directly upgrade to the EMR 7’s default java version.
